turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
add the refernces within the text:Digital transformation (DT) has emerged as a critical capability for the survival of hospitals, especially given the impact of the Covid-19 pandemic. However, there is a significant dearth of empirical research on what drives this transformation, creating a knowledge gap. Although practitioners suggest that IT capabilities play a part in DT, the literature lacks empirical evidence and consensus on their specific dimensions and roles. According to the resource-based view (RBV), IT capabilities are a source of competitive advantage that is not easily replicated by competitors, unlike IT resources, which may become standardized over time. While previous studies have often viewed physical IT infrastructure as a capability, the rapid expansion of digital technologies, such as cloud computing, raises questions about whether this traditional perspective remains accurate in the healthcare context, particularly during the DT process. To address this gap, this study develops an empirical model, based on the RBV perspective, to explore the role of physical IT infrastructure in enabling DT in hospitals.

References:

- Ahuja, V., & Thatcher, S. M. (2019). The Evolution of Digital Transformation: A Review of the Literature. In Digital Transformation and Global Society (pp. 1-14). Springer, Cham.
- Fernandes, L. L., & Faria, A. (2021). Digital health transformation in times of COVID-19: Discourses and challenges. Journal of Health Management, 23(4), 525-534.
- Gyamfi, D. J., & Boso, N. S. (2020). Digital transformation and firm performance in developing countries: a resource-based view approach. Management Decision, 58(3), 476-492.
- Hsu, S. H., Chuang, H. M., & Hsu, Y. L. (2022). Business model innovation and digital transformation in healthcare industry: A resource-based view perspective. Technological Forecasting and Social Change, 173, 121028.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text: Digital transformation (DT) has emerged as a critical capability for the survival of hospitals, especially given the impact of the Covid-19 pandemic (Fernandes & Faria, 2021). However, there is a significant dearth of empirical research on what drives this transformation, creating a knowledge gap. Although practitioners suggest that IT capabilities play a part in DT, the literature lacks empirical evidence and consensus on their specific dimensions and roles (Ahuja & Thatcher, 2019). According to the resource-based view (RBV), IT capabilities are a source of competitive advantage that is not easily replicated by competitors, unlike IT resources, which may become standardized over time (Gyamfi & Boso, 2020). While previous studies have often viewed physical IT infrastructure as a capability, the rapid expansion of digital technologies, such as cloud computing, raises questions about whether this traditional perspective remains accurate in the healthcare context, particularly during the DT process. To address this gap, this study develops an empirical model, based on the RBV perspective, to explore the role of physical IT infrastructure in enabling DT in hospitals (Hsu et al., 2022).

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: edit: The study contribution was twofold: First, Physical IT infrastructure has long been viewed as a dimensions of IT capability in most studies, this study argues that the exponential growth of digital technologies, including cloud computing, has led to change this traditional viewpoint in the context of hospitals, particularly during the process of digital transformation. in this study Physical IT infrastructure is considered as a “IT resource” while IT architecture, IT human capability, and IT relationship capability are considered as the dimensions of hospital-specific IT capabilities. Second, while the physical IT infrastructure as a platform and shared resource in a hospital supports the dimensions of IT capabilities, it is not able to directly influence digital transformation, while is influencing digital transformation through IT capabilities dimensions and strategic alignment.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Edited:

The contribution of this study was twofold. First, previous studies have traditionally viewed physical IT infrastructure as a dimension of IT capability. However, the exponential growth of digital technologies, including cloud computing, has led to a change in this viewpoint, particularly in the context of hospitals during the process of digital transformation. In this study, physical IT infrastructure is considered an "IT resource", while the dimensions of hospital-specific IT capabilities are IT architecture, IT human capability, and IT relationship capability. Second, while physical IT infrastructure in a hospital supports the dimensions of IT capabilities as a platform and shared resource, it does not have a direct influence on digital transformation. Instead, it influences digital transformation through IT capability dimensions and strategic alignment.

Revised:

This study makes a twofold contribution. First, previous studies have traditionally viewed physical IT infrastructure as a dimension of IT capability, but this study argues that the exponential growth of digital technologies, including cloud computing, has led to a change in this viewpoint, particularly in the context of hospitals during the process of digital transformation. In this study, physical IT infrastructure is considered an "IT resource", while the dimensions of hospital-specific IT capabilities are IT architecture, IT human capability, and IT relationship capability. Second, while physical IT infrastructure in a hospital supports the dimensions of IT capabilities as a platform and shared resource, it does not have a direct influence on digital transformation. Instead, it influences digital transformation indirectly through IT capability dimensions and strategic alignment.
